enrichment.short_description: AI-assisted RSS reader that automatically tags and scores news entries so you only see relevant stories.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Feeds Fun is an open-source news reader built around automatic tagging and scoring rather than folders and manual sorting. Every incoming entry is run through a tagging pipeline, and users write scoring rules that boost or bury stories based on the tags they contain, turning a large firehose of feeds into a ranked, filtered stream.

The project pairs a Python backend (distributed as the `ffun` package) with a Vue/TypeScript frontend. A free hosted instance is available at feeds.fun with curated feed collections, and the same software can be self-hosted via Docker in single-user or multi-user configurations.

Tagging and relevance scoring are powered by large language models: Feeds Fun supports OpenAI (ChatGPT) and Google Gemini APIs, as well as other providers through OpenAI-compatible or Gemini-compatible endpoints. Users supply their own API key, and the LLM cost is billed directly by the model provider rather than by Feeds Fun.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: People who follow a high volume of feeds — Reddit, GitHub, YouTube, arXiv, Hacker News, and general RSS/Atom sources — and want automated relevance filtering instead of manually triaging an inbox-style reader.

features.title: Automatic AI tagging
features.description: Incoming entries are automatically tagged using a connected LLM (OpenAI, Gemini, or compatible endpoints).

features.title: Custom scoring rules
features.description: Users write rules that raise or lower an entry's score based on its tags, effectively creating a personalized relevance filter.

features.title: Curated source support
features.description: Improved handling for major feed sources including Reddit, GitHub, YouTube, arXiv, and Hacker News.

features.title: Free hosted instance
features.description: A hosted version at feeds.fun is available at no cost, with users supplying their own LLM API key.

features.title: Self-hosted Docker deployment
features.description: Single-user and multi-user Docker setups are documented for running Feeds Fun on your own infrastructure.

features.title: Multi-user support
features.description: Supports both personal, single-user setups and shared multi-user deployments.

pros: Free to self-host or use on the hosted instance
AI tagging removes manual folder/category upkeep
Custom scoring rules give fine-grained control over what surfaces
Works well with high-volume aggregator sources like Reddit and Hacker News
cons: Requires a personal LLM API key (OpenAI or Gemini) for tagging to work, which adds an external cost and dependency
Smaller community and less documentation than mainstream readers like FreshRSS or Miniflux
AI tagging quality depends on the chosen LLM provider and prompt tuning

faqs.question: Is Feeds Fun free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the software is open source and free. The hosted instance at feeds.fun is also free to use, though you need to supply your own OpenAI or Gemini API key for AI tagging.

faqs.question: Do I need an OpenAI account to use Feeds Fun?
faqs.answer: To get AI-powered tagging and scoring you need an API key from OpenAI, Google Gemini, or another OpenAI/Gemini-compatible provider. The reader itself will still run without one, but the tagging feature depends on it.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Feeds Fun?
faqs.answer: Yes, Feeds Fun can be deployed via Docker in single-user or multi-user configurations, in addition to the free hosted instance.

use_cases.title: Filtering high-volume tech feeds
use_cases.description: Automatically tag and rank a large volume of Hacker News, Reddit, and GitHub activity so only the highest-scoring items surface.

use_cases.title: Personal research feed
use_cases.description: Track arXiv and blog feeds with custom scoring rules tuned to specific research interests.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Feeds Fun is an open-source RSS reader that uses AI tagging and custom scoring rules to filter feeds. Free hosted instance or self-host via Docker.

enrichment.short_description: A free, self-hostable RSS and Atom feed aggregator with search, filters, extensions, and multi-user support.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: FreshRSS is a self-hosted feed aggregator for following websites, podcasts, and video channels from RSS and Atom sources in one place. It ships with a built-in article reader, full-text search, and the ability to generate custom feeds by scraping websites that don't publish RSS natively.

The project is licensed under AGPL 3 and is designed to run efficiently even at large scale, with the project stating it can handle over 1 million articles and 50,000 feeds without slowing down. FreshRSS supports OPML import/export, WebSub for near-real-time updates, and a plugin/extension and theming system for customization.
Because FreshRSS exposes API endpoints compatible with the Google Reader and Fever APIs, it works with a large ecosystem of third-party mobile apps rather than requiring an official native client.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Self-hosters who want full control over their reading data and are comfortable running a lightweight PHP application on their own server, whether for personal use or a small shared/multi-user instance.

features.title: RSS/Atom aggregation
features.description: Follow websites, podcasts, and video channels through standard feed formats in a single reader.

features.title: Built-in search and article reader
features.description: Full-text search across subscribed feeds and a distraction-free reading view.

features.title: Website scraping for feed generation
features.description: Generate feeds for sites that don't natively publish RSS/Atom.

features.title: Custom filtered feeds
features.description: Create saved search feeds based on filter rules across subscriptions.

features.title: OPML import/export
features.description: Migrate subscriptions in and out using the standard OPML format.

features.title: Extensions and themes
features.description: Extend functionality and customize the interface through an extensions ecosystem.

features.title: Third-party mobile app support
features.description: Google Reader and Fever API compatibility lets FreshRSS work with many existing RSS mobile apps.

features.title: Multi-user support
features.description: Runs as a shared instance for multiple accounts on one server.

pros: Completely free, open-source, and self-hosted with no account limits
Handles very large numbers of feeds and articles efficiently
Broad mobile app compatibility via Google Reader/Fever API support
Active extension ecosystem and translated into many languages
cons: Requires self-hosting; no official managed cloud version
No official first-party mobile app — depends on third-party clients
Initial server setup (PHP, database) needed before use

faqs.question: Is FreshRSS free?
faqs.answer: Yes, FreshRSS is free and open source under the AGPL 3 license. It has no paid tiers; you self-host it on your own server.

faqs.question: Does FreshRSS have a mobile app?
faqs.answer: There's no single official native app, but FreshRSS supports the Google Reader and Fever APIs, so it works with many third-party RSS mobile apps.

faqs.question: Can I try FreshRSS before installing it?
faqs.answer: A public demo is available at demo.freshrss.org.

faqs.question: Can multiple people use one FreshRSS install?
faqs.answer: Yes, FreshRSS supports multiple user accounts on a single self-hosted instance.

use_cases.title: Personal RSS hub
use_cases.description: Consolidate blogs, podcasts, and video channels into a single self-hosted reading list.

use_cases.title: Shared team/family feed server
use_cases.description: Run one FreshRSS instance with multiple user accounts for a household or small team.

use_cases.title: Feed generation for non-RSS sites
use_cases.description: Scrape websites lacking native RSS to build custom feeds and filtered views.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: FreshRSS is a free, self-hosted RSS and Atom feed aggregator with search, filters, extensions, and support for large feed collections.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted RSS/Atom feed aggregator with a three-column reading interface and readability-based article cleanup.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: JARR (Just Another RSS Reader) is a self-hosted web application for aggregating RSS and Atom feeds into a single reading interface. It presents subscriptions in a three-column layout — feed/category list, article list, and content view — and supports folding feeds into categories.

The application includes readability-style cleanup to strip poorly formatted article markup and a crawler with a queue system built to fetch large numbers of feeds without excessive resource use. It's built on Python, Flask, SQLAlchemy, and PostgreSQL, with a ReactJS frontend, and has a mobile-responsive layout for phones and tablets.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Technically comfortable self-hosters who want a lightweight, self-run alternative to hosted feed readers and are comfortable deploying a Python/PostgreSQL application themselves.

features.title: Three-column reading interface
features.description: Feeds/categories, article list, and content viewer are shown side by side for fast browsing.

features.title: Feed categorization
features.description: Group feeds into categories with the ability to fold/collapse them.

features.title: Readability integration
features.description: Cleans up poorly formatted article content for a more consistent reading view.

features.title: Queued crawler
features.description: A crawler with a queue system fetches feed updates designed to scale without heavy resource use.

features.title: Mobile-responsive design
features.description: Interface adapts for use on phones and tablets.

pros: Free, open-source, and self-hosted
Lightweight crawler designed to handle many feeds efficiently
Readability cleanup improves articles from poorly formatted sources
Mobile-responsive interface works without a separate app
cons: Smaller community and less active development than FreshRSS, Miniflux, or Tiny Tiny RSS
Requires manual PostgreSQL/Python setup to self-host
No official mobile app or hosted service

faqs.question: Is JARR free?
faqs.answer: Yes, JARR is free, open-source software that you self-host.

faqs.question: What does JARR require to run?
faqs.answer: JARR is built on Python 3, Flask, SQLAlchemy, and PostgreSQL, so it requires a server capable of running a Python application with a PostgreSQL database.

faqs.question: Does JARR have a mobile app?
faqs.answer: There is no dedicated native mobile app; JARR's web interface is mobile-responsive for use on phones and tablets.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted personal feed reader
use_cases.description: Run a private RSS/Atom aggregator on your own server with a compact three-column interface.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: JARR is a free, self-hosted RSS and Atom feed reader with a three-column interface, feed categorization, and readability-based article cleanup.

enrichment.short_description: Minimalist, privacy-focused RSS and Atom feed reader written in Go, self-hostable or available as a low-cost hosted service.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Miniflux is a minimalist feed reader written in Go, built by developer Frédéric Guillot around the philosophy that "the content is the most important thing, everything else is just noise." It strips visual clutter in favor of a fast, keyboard-driven reading experience.

The software is free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license, distributed as a single static binary with Docker images and RPM/Debian packages for self-hosting. Miniflux automatically fetches full article content for feeds that only publish summaries, strips tracking pixels and URL tracking parameters, and proxies media to protect reader privacy — the project states it collects no telemetry.
For people who don't want to self-host, Miniflux also offers a managed hosted version priced at $15/year with a 15-day free trial.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Readers who want a fast, no-frills, privacy-respecting feed reader — either self-hosted for full control, or via the inexpensive hosted plan for those who don't want to run their own server.

features.title: Full-article fetching
features.description: Automatically retrieves full article content for feeds that only publish summaries.

features.title: Privacy protections
features.description: Strips tracking pixels and URL tracking parameters and proxies media; the project states it collects no telemetry.

features.title: Keyboard shortcuts
features.description: Navigate and triage articles quickly without touching the mouse.

features.title: Single static binary
features.description: Simple installation with a static Go binary, Docker image, or RPM/Debian package.

features.title: Minimalist reading interface
features.description: Deliberately stripped-down UI focused on article content over visual chrome.

features.title: Low-cost hosted option
features.description: A managed hosted service is available for $15/year with a 15-day free trial for those who don't want to self-host.

pros: Free and open source for self-hosting, with a very low-cost hosted alternative
Fast, lightweight, and easy to deploy as a single binary or Docker container
Strong privacy defaults: no telemetry, tracker/pixel stripping, media proxying
Full-article fetching improves summary-only feeds
cons: Minimalist interface may feel bare compared to feature-rich readers like Tiny Tiny RSS
No official native mobile app (relies on third-party or web clients)
Hosted plan is a paid add-on rather than a free managed tier

faqs.question: Is Miniflux free?
faqs.answer: The self-hosted software is free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license. Miniflux also offers an optional managed hosted service for $15/year with a 15-day trial.

faqs.question: Who created Miniflux?
faqs.answer: Miniflux is developed by Frédéric Guillot, with source code publicly available on GitHub.

faqs.question: Does Miniflux track users?
faqs.answer: The project states it removes tracking pixels and URL tracking parameters, proxies media, and does not collect telemetry.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted minimalist reading setup
use_cases.description: Deploy a lightweight, distraction-free feed reader on your own server using Docker or a static binary.

use_cases.title: Low-maintenance hosted reading
use_cases.description: Use the $15/year hosted plan to get a privacy-respecting reader without managing a server.
use_cases.audience: Individuals who don't want to self-host

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Miniflux is a minimalist, privacy-focused RSS reader written in Go. Free and self-hosted, or hosted for $15/year with a 15-day trial.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source RSS reader that trains on your likes and dislikes to filter stories, with free, paid, and self-hosted options.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: NewsBlur is a cloud-based feed reader that emerged as one of the well-known alternatives after Google Reader shut down. Beyond standard RSS/Atom feeds, it supports "web feeds" for sites without RSS and email newsletter subscriptions, and it groups duplicate coverage of the same story from multiple sources.

Its signature feature is 'Intelligence Training': users can hide stories they don't like and highlight ones they do, and NewsBlur applies that training to filter future stories — without relying on an opaque algorithmic feed. Native apps exist for iOS and Android, and the reader is also compatible with third-party RSS apps such as Reeder, ReadKit, and Unread.
NewsBlur is open source under the MIT license and can be self-hosted via Docker, giving users the option of full data ownership instead of using the hosted service.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Readers who want a trainable, non-algorithmic alternative to hosted readers, from casual users on the free plan to power users who want full-text search, an article archive, and higher feed limits on paid tiers.

features.title: Intelligence Training
features.description: Hide or highlight stories to teach NewsBlur which content to surface, without an opaque algorithm.

features.title: Story clustering
features.description: Groups duplicate coverage of the same story from different sources.

features.title: Full-text search
features.description: Search across subscribed feeds (available on paid plans).

features.title: Web feeds and newsletters
features.description: Follow sites without native RSS and receive email newsletters inside the reader.

features.title: Native mobile apps
features.description: Dedicated iOS and Android apps, plus compatibility with third-party RSS clients.

features.title: Self-hosting option
features.description: Open-source under the MIT license with a documented single-command Docker self-hosting path.

pros: Free tier available with no credit card required for a 30-day premium trial
Open source (MIT license) with a self-hosting option for full data ownership
Non-algorithmic training-based filtering keeps user control over what's surfaced
Native iOS and Android apps plus broad third-party app compatibility
cons: Free plan is capped at 64 sites, which is limiting for heavy feed followers
Full feature set (search, archive, fast fetching) requires a paid plan
Self-hosting requires running your own Docker infrastructure to match hosted service features

faqs.question: Is NewsBlur free?
faqs.answer: Yes, there is a free plan limited to 64 sites. Paid plans (Premium, Premium Archive, Premium Pro) raise site limits and unlock features like search and faster fetching.

faqs.question: How much does NewsBlur Premium cost?
faqs.answer: Premium is $36/year (1,024 sites), Premium Archive is $99/year (4,096 sites plus full archive and AI features), and Premium Pro is $29/month (10,000 sites with 5-15 minute fetching).

faqs.question: Can I self-host NewsBlur?
faqs.answer: Yes, NewsBlur is open source under the MIT license and can be self-hosted via Docker with a documented setup process.

faqs.question: Does NewsBlur have mobile apps?
faqs.answer: Yes, native apps are available for iOS and Android, and NewsBlur is also compatible with third-party apps like Reeder, ReadKit, and Unread.

use_cases.title: Post-Google Reader feed following
use_cases.description: Follow blogs and news sites with a trainable reader instead of an algorithmic feed.

use_cases.title: Heavy feed consumption
use_cases.description: Use a paid plan to track thousands of sites with full-text search and a story archive.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted data ownership
use_cases.description: Run NewsBlur's open-source Docker deployment to keep reading data entirely on your own infrastructure.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: NewsBlur is an open-source RSS reader that trains on your likes and dislikes to filter stories. Free plan, paid tiers, and self-hosting available.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted web news aggregator for managing RSS/Atom feeds, bookmarks, and notes with multi-user and API support.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Newspipe is a self-hosted web news aggregator built by developer Cédric Bonhomme, described in its own README as letting users "own your feeds, your data, and your reading." It's built on Flask, asyncio, and SQLAlchemy, running on Python 3.13+, and can be deployed with either SQLite or PostgreSQL.

Beyond core feed reading, Newspipe supports multi-user accounts, a programmatic API, JSON export/import for data portability, OPML feed management, full-text search, bookmarking with notes, optional LDAP authentication, and detection of inactive feeds. It also supports importing bookmarks from Pinboard. The interface is available in English, French, and German with light and dark themes.

The project is licensed under the GNU Affero General Public License v3 and remains actively maintained, with source hosted on SourceHut (and mirrored on GitHub) and feed fetching handled via scheduled cron jobs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Privacy-focused self-hosters who want a combined feed reader and bookmarking tool with API access, running on their own Python/PostgreSQL or SQLite server.

features.title: RSS/Atom aggregation
features.description: Aggregates feeds into a self-hosted reading interface.

features.title: Multi-user accounts
features.description: Supports multiple independent user accounts on one instance.

features.title: Programmatic API
features.description: Exposes an API for interacting with feeds and articles programmatically.

features.title: Bookmarks and notes
features.description: Save bookmarks with personal notes, including import from Pinboard.

features.title: JSON data export/import
features.description: Full data portability through JSON export and import.

features.title: OPML management
features.description: Import and export feed subscriptions in OPML format.

features.title: Optional LDAP authentication
features.description: Can authenticate users against an existing LDAP directory.

pros: Free, open-source, and self-hosted with an active project maintainer
Combines feed reading with bookmarking and notes in one tool
Data portability via JSON export/import and OPML
Multi-user and LDAP support suit small shared deployments
cons: Requires Python 3.13+ and manual server setup; no official managed hosting
Smaller community than FreshRSS, Miniflux, or Tiny Tiny RSS
No native mobile app

faqs.question: Is Newspipe free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Newspipe is free, open-source software under the AGPLv3 license.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Newspipe?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's designed to be self-hosted, built on Flask/asyncio/SQLAlchemy with SQLite or PostgreSQL, and deployed via git, Poetry, and npm.

faqs.question: Does Newspipe support multiple users?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports multi-user accounts on a single instance, plus optional LDAP authentication.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted feeds and bookmarks in one tool
use_cases.description: Combine RSS reading with bookmark saving and notes on your own server.

use_cases.title: API-driven feed workflows
use_cases.description: Use Newspipe's API to build custom tooling or automation around feed data.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Newspipe is a free, self-hosted RSS/Atom news aggregator with bookmarking, notes, multi-user accounts, and an API, built on Flask and SQLAlchemy.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted read-it-later and news reader with offline article saving, categorization, and webhook integrations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Readflow is a read-it-later and news-reading application built by developer Nicolas Carlier, focused on versatility and simplicity. Articles can be saved for offline reading or exported locally in formats like HTML, PDF, EPUB, or ZIP, and organized into categories with automatic classification.

The tool connects to external services through incoming and outgoing webhooks, supporting sources and destinations such as RSS, Pocket, Shaarli, Wallabag, and S3-compatible storage, plus a scripting engine for customizing how articles are processed. It's written in Go and licensed under AGPL-3.0, and can be installed via Go, a precompiled binary, or Docker/Docker Compose.
Readflow is delivered as a Progressive Web App, giving it an app-like experience on mobile without a native app store listing, and it includes push notifications for new articles.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Self-hosters who want a read-it-later workflow with webhook-based integrations into other tools (Wallabag, Shaarli, Pocket, RSS sources) rather than a traditional folder-based RSS reader.

features.title: Offline article saving
features.description: Save articles for offline reading or export them locally in HTML, PDF, EPUB, or ZIP formats.

features.title: Automatic categorization
features.description: Classify incoming articles into categories automatically.

features.title: Incoming/outgoing webhooks
features.description: Integrate with services like RSS, Pocket, Shaarli, Wallabag, and S3-compatible storage.

features.title: Scripting engine
features.description: Customize article processing behavior with scripts.

features.title: Progressive Web App
features.description: App-like mobile experience without a native app store install.

features.title: Ad- and tracker-free
features.description: The reader operates without ads or trackers.

pros: Free, open source, and self-hosted under AGPL-3.0
Flexible webhook integrations connect it to other self-hosted tools
Multiple export formats for offline reading (HTML, PDF, EPUB, ZIP)
PWA support gives a native-app-like mobile experience without a store listing
cons: Requires self-hosting via Go binary or Docker; no managed hosted plan
Smaller community than mainstream RSS readers
No dedicated native iOS/Android app (PWA only)

faqs.question: Is Readflow free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Readflow is free, open-source software licensed under AGPL-3.0.

faqs.question: Is Readflow an RSS reader or a read-it-later app?
faqs.answer: It combines both: it can ingest RSS feeds via webhooks and functions as a read-it-later tool with offline saving, categorization, and export.

faqs.question: Does Readflow have a mobile app?
faqs.answer: There's no native app store listing; Readflow is a Progressive Web App that provides an app-like mobile experience with push notifications.

use_cases.title: Read-it-later with offline export
use_cases.description: Save articles from feeds and other sources for offline reading in HTML, PDF, or EPUB.

use_cases.title: Webhook-connected reading pipeline
use_cases.description: Route articles between Readflow, Wallabag, Shaarli, Pocket, and S3 storage using webhooks.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Readflow is a free, self-hosted read-it-later and news reader with offline export, categorization, and webhook integrations.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source RSS feed generator that creates feeds for thousands of sites and services that don't natively offer RSS.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: RSSHub describes itself as "the world's largest RSS network," generating RSS feeds for websites and services that don't natively publish them. It ships with a very large library of pre-configured routes covering social platforms, news sites, video sites, and more, so users can plug a target URL or ID into a route and get a working feed without writing any code.

The project is open source under the AGPL-3.0 license and is community-driven, with the project stating over 1,300 contributors and maintainers actively supporting it. RSSHub can be used through public instances run by the community, or self-hosted for reliability and to avoid rate limits, and it exposes APIs for developers building on top of it.
RSSHub integrates with the broader RSS ecosystem, including feed readers like Folo, which the RSSHub docs describe as an AI RSS reader built for smarter feed discovery.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Developers and power users who want to turn non-RSS sources (social feeds, marketplaces, forums, and more) into standard RSS/Atom feeds for use in any feed reader.

features.title: Thousands of pre-configured routes
features.description: Ready-made feed generators for a wide range of sites and services that lack native RSS support.

features.title: Public instances
features.description: Community-run public instances let users try routes without deploying their own server.

features.title: Self-hosted deployment
features.description: Run your own RSSHub instance for reliability, custom routes, and to avoid shared rate limits.

features.title: Open API and ecosystem
features.description: Documented APIs support integration with feed readers and other tooling.

features.title: Large contributor community
features.description: Over 1,300 contributors maintain and extend the route library.

pros: Free and open source with a very large library of ready-made feed routes
Public instances let you get started without any setup
Self-hosting option avoids shared rate limits and adds custom routes
Large, active contributor community keeps routes updated
cons: Public instances can be rate-limited or occasionally unstable, since they're community-run
Some routes depend on third-party site structures and can break when a source site changes
Self-hosting for reliability requires ongoing server maintenance

faqs.question: Is RSSHub free?
faqs.answer: Yes, RSSHub is free and open source under the AGPL-3.0 license.

faqs.question: Do I need to run my own RSSHub server?
faqs.answer: No, community-run public instances are available for immediate use, but self-hosting is recommended for more reliable, rate-limit-free access.

faqs.question: What does RSSHub actually generate feeds from?
faqs.answer: RSSHub has pre-configured routes for a very large number of sites and services that don't publish RSS natively, converting their content into standard RSS/Atom feeds.

use_cases.title: Following non-RSS sources
use_cases.description: Generate an RSS feed for a social account, marketplace listing, or site that has no native feed.

use_cases.title: Feed infrastructure for developers
use_cases.description: Self-host RSSHub as backend infrastructure feeding other tools or a custom reader.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: RSSHub is an open-source RSS feed generator with pre-configured routes for thousands of sites that don't natively support RSS.

enrichment.short_description: Lightweight, self-hosted PHP web application for aggregating RSS feeds and other content sources into one reader.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Selfoss is a self-hosted, PHP-based web application that describes itself as "the ultimate multi-purpose RSS reader, data stream, mash-up, aggregation web application," consolidating RSS feeds and other content sources into a single interface. It's a small, roughly 25 MB install designed to be simple to deploy — the project describes setup as "just upload and run."

It supports MySQL, PostgreSQL, or SQLite as a database backend, includes a plug-in system for extensibility, OPML import for migrating subscriptions, and a RESTful JSON API for building custom clients or integrations. A responsive layout adapts to desktop, tablet, and mobile browsers, and a third-party Android app is available through F-Droid.
Selfoss is free and open source, with source code hosted on GitHub, and is currently at version 2.19.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Self-hosters looking for a lightweight, easy-to-deploy PHP feed aggregator, especially those already running a standard LAMP-style hosting stack.

features.title: Lightweight PHP install
features.description: A roughly 25 MB application designed for simple 'upload and run' deployment.

features.title: Multiple database backends
features.description: Works with MySQL, PostgreSQL, or SQLite.

features.title: Plug-in system
features.description: Extend functionality through plug-ins.

features.title: OPML import
features.description: Migrate existing feed subscriptions into Selfoss.

features.title: RESTful JSON API
features.description: Programmatic access for building custom clients or integrations.

features.title: Responsive interface
features.description: Adapts to desktop, tablet, and mobile browsers.

features.title: Third-party Android app
features.description: An Android client is available via F-Droid.

pros: Free, open source, and very lightweight to self-host
Simple deployment on standard PHP hosting with multiple database options
RESTful JSON API enables custom clients and integrations
Third-party Android app available through F-Droid
cons: No official native mobile app; Android support comes from a third-party client
Requires self-managed PHP hosting; no managed cloud option
Interface and feature set are more basic than some full-featured alternatives

faqs.question: Is Selfoss free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Selfoss is free and open-source software that you self-host.

faqs.question: What databases does Selfoss support?
faqs.answer: Selfoss can run on MySQL, PostgreSQL, or SQLite.

faqs.question: Does Selfoss have a mobile app?
faqs.answer: There is a third-party Android app available via F-Droid; the web interface is also responsive for mobile browsers.

use_cases.title: Lightweight self-hosted aggregator
use_cases.description: Deploy a small-footprint PHP feed reader on existing shared or LAMP hosting.

use_cases.title: Custom feed client development

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Selfoss is a free, lightweight, self-hosted PHP RSS reader and content aggregator with a plug-in system and RESTful JSON API.

enrichment.short_description: Free, self-hosted RSS/Atom news aggregator with plugins, filtering, deduplication, and an API, deployed via Docker.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Tiny Tiny RSS (tt-rss) is a free, self-hosted web-based news aggregator for reading RSS, Atom, and other feed formats. It's built to let users "control your own data and protect your privacy instead of relying on third party services," and organizes feeds hierarchically into folders and subfolders.

The project supports article filtering and deduplication (including perceptual image hashing to catch visually duplicate posts), podcast feed support, a JSON API for integrations, and a plugin and theme system for customization. It's licensed under GNU GPLv3 and Docker deployment is the recommended and fully supported installation path.
Tiny Tiny RSS follows a continuous development model where the main branch is considered stable, and users are advised to keep their installations reasonably current with recent updates.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Self-hosters who want a fully-featured, extensible feed reader with folders, filtering, and an API, and who are comfortable running and maintaining a Docker-based service.

features.title: Hierarchical feed organization
features.description: Organize subscriptions into folders and subfolders.

features.title: Article filtering and deduplication
features.description: Filter incoming articles and detect duplicates, including perceptual image hashing for visual duplicates.

features.title: Plugin and theme system
features.description: Customize behavior and appearance through plugins and themes.

features.title: JSON API
features.description: Programmatic access for building integrations and third-party clients.

features.title: Podcast support
features.description: Handles podcast feeds alongside standard article feeds.

features.title: OPML import/export
features.description: Migrate subscriptions using the standard OPML format.

features.title: Keyboard shortcuts
features.description: Navigate and manage feeds efficiently without a mouse.

pros: Free, open source (GPLv3), and self-hosted with no account limits
Extensive plugin ecosystem and customizable theming
Robust article filtering and duplicate detection
JSON API supports third-party clients and integrations
cons: Docker is the recommended deployment path, adding setup overhead for non-Docker users
No official managed hosted service
Continuous main-branch development model means less traditional versioned releases

faqs.question: Is Tiny Tiny RSS free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Tiny Tiny RSS is free, open-source software under the GNU GPLv3 license, self-hosted on your own server.

faqs.question: What's the recommended way to install Tiny Tiny RSS?
faqs.answer: Docker deployment is the recommended and fully supported installation method.

faqs.question: Does Tiny Tiny RSS have an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, it provides a JSON API for building integrations and third-party clients.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted feed reader with plugins
use_cases.description: Run a customizable, plugin-extensible RSS/Atom reader entirely on your own infrastructure.

use_cases.title: API-driven feed client development
use_cases.description: Build custom or mobile clients against tt-rss's JSON API.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Tiny Tiny RSS is a free, self-hosted RSS/Atom news aggregator with plugins, filtering, deduplication, and a JSON API.
